items.prompt_md: In the product-except-self solution, why does the algorithm build both a **prefix** running product and a **suffix** running product?
options: Because the array might be sorted in either direction
Because each index's answer needs everything before it combined with everything after it, and neither alone is enough
Because dividing by zero is faster with two passes
Because a single pass cannot visit every index
items.answer: Because each index's answer needs everything before it combined with everything after it, and neither alone is enough
items.explanation_md: Index i's answer is "product of everything except nums[i]," which splits cleanly into "everything before i" (prefix) and "everything after i" (suffix) — you need both halves combined.

items.prompt_md: You must answer, for every index of a fixed array, a question that combines all elements before and after that index, without using division. Which approach fits?
options: Precompute running prefix and suffix totals once, then combine them per index in O(1)
For each index, rescan the whole array skipping that index
Keep a hash map of values seen so far and look up each complement
Sort the array and use two pointers to shrink a window
items.answer: Precompute running prefix and suffix totals once, then combine them per index in O(1)
items.explanation_md: "Same combine-a-range question, asked for every index, array doesn't change" is the fingerprint for prefix sums/products: one O(n) precompute turns every per-index query into O(1).

items.prompt_md: What is the time complexity of the two-pass prefix/suffix solution to Product of Array Except Self, for an array of length n?
items.explanation_md: Building the prefix pass is O(n) and the suffix pass is O(n); two linear passes over the array is still O(n) overall, versus O(n^2) for recomputing each index's product from scratch.
